description: This figure presents the mechanical behavior and underlying deformation mechanisms of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density steel processed via homogenization at 1150°C for 4 h, hot-rolling to 16.5 mm diameter rods, and solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h with oil quenching. The as-processed microstructure consists of ~97% equiaxed austenite (γ, FCC, 10.8 μm grain size) with ~3% ferrite (α, BCC) at grain boundaries. The figure likely displays stress-strain curves comparing room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, -196°C) deformation, showing yield strength increasing from 251±3 MPa (RT) to 497±5 MPa (LNT), ultimate tensile strength from 580±6 MPa to 945±8 MPa, and total elongation from 52.0±0.5% to 75.5±0.5%. Interrupted tensile tests reveal that deformation twins first nucleate at ~28% strain at RT and as early as ~10% strain at LNT, with extensive primary and secondary twinning observed at 55% strain at LNT. The work hardening behavior differs between temperatures: RT shows three stages while LNT exhibits four stages including a distinctive stage B with nearly constant hardening rate attributed to twin nucleation at annealed twin boundaries. Despite a high stacking fault energy (62.64 mJ/m² at RT, 49.46 mJ/m² at LNT), deformation twinning activates due to planar slip induced by short-range ordering (L1₂) and stress concentration at cell block walls, validating the steel's superior cryogenic toughness (254 J at LNT vs 331 J at RT) through sustained work hardening mechanisms.
